questions.text: What is the syntax for a Terraform resource block?
questions.explanation: Terraform resource blocks use the syntax: resource "resource_type" "local_name" { arguments }. The type (e.g., aws_s3_bucket) determines which provider's API is called. The local name is used to reference the resource elsewhere in the configuration.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a data source in Terraform?

options.text: To read information from existing infrastructure without managing it

questions.explanation: Data sources allow Terraform to fetch and use information defined outside of Terraform (existing AWS resources, remote state, external APIs). They are read-only — Terraform never creates or modifies data sources. Reference them with data.type.name.attribute.

questions.text: What is an implicit dependency in Terraform?

options.text: A dependency inferred by Terraform when one resource references another's attributes
A provider dependency declared in required_providers
A module dependency declared in module blocks

questions.explanation: Terraform infers implicit dependencies when a resource references another resource's attributes (e.g., vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id). Terraform builds a dependency graph from these references and creates resources in the correct order. Explicit depends_on is only needed for non-attribute dependencies.

questions.text: When should you use 'for_each' instead of 'count'?

options.text: When you need more than 5 resources
When resources are distinguished by meaningful keys (not just index numbers)
for_each is always better than count
When the number of resources is not known at plan time

questions.explanation: for_each is preferred when each resource has a meaningful unique key (e.g., bucket purpose, environment name). This makes state addresses like aws_s3_bucket.app["logs"] more stable than aws_s3_bucket.app[2]. Removing an item from a for_each set doesn't shift indices like count does.

questions.text: How do you reference the ID of resource 'aws_vpc.main' in another resource?
questions.explanation: Resources are referenced with resource_type.resource_name.attribute (e.g., aws_vpc.main.id). No 'resource.' prefix is needed for resource references. Data sources use data.type.name.attribute. This reference syntax creates implicit dependencies.

options.text: Declares explicit dependencies that Terraform can't infer from attributes
Specifies which provider version is required
Controls the order of output values

questions.explanation: depends_on creates explicit ordering dependencies when Terraform can't infer the dependency through attribute references (e.g., when one resource relies on a side-effect of another, like an IAM policy attachment). Use it sparingly — prefer implicit dependencies through attribute references when possible.

questions.text: With 'count = 3' on a resource, how do you get the current iteration index?
questions.explanation: When using count, each instance has access to count.index (0-based integer). Use it to create unique resource names: Name = "web-${count.index}". Instances are addressed as resource_type.name[0], resource_type.name[1], etc.

questions.text: What does 'toset()' do in a for_each expression?

options.text: Converts a map to a set
Converts a list to a set (removes duplicates, enables for_each)

questions.explanation: toset() converts a list to a set, which removes duplicates and unorders elements. for_each requires either a set of strings or a map — it cannot directly use a list. toset(["a", "b", "c"]) produces a set where each.key equals the string value.

questions.text: What is the 'lifecycle' meta-argument used for in Terraform?

options.text: Controlling create/destroy behavior: create_before_destroy, prevent_destroy, ignore_changes
Specifying which provider lifecycle to use

questions.explanation: The lifecycle block controls how Terraform handles resource creation and destruction. Key options: create_before_destroy (creates new before deleting old), prevent_destroy (prevents terraform destroy from deleting), ignore_changes (ignores drift in specified attributes), and replace_triggered_by.

questions.text: What is a 'for expression' in Terraform used for?

options.text: Looping over resources to create multiple instances
Transforming one collection type into another (list to map, filtering)
Creating for loops in template files

questions.explanation: For expressions transform collections: [for s in var.names : upper(s)] produces a list, {for k, v in map : k => v.id} produces a map. They can also filter with if: [for s in list : s if s != ""]. Used in outputs, locals, and variable defaults.
